title: Prepare an input clip
summary: Use this rail to set the stages that feed the full detector -> tracking -> calibration -> overlay path.
body: This workspace is for short, iterative setup on one broadcast clip at a time. The goal is to make the pipeline legible before you trust longer runs or downstream summaries.

A bad choice here usually fails downstream in a recognizable order: weak detection poisons tracking, weak tracking makes team separation noisy, and poor field registration makes the minimap or projection unreliable even if the boxes look reasonable.

title: Analysis pipeline
summary: Selects the detection and field-registration architecture that drives the entire analysis run.
body: Classic uses the soccana detector for player/ball detection plus soccana_keypoint for field registration. These are separate models with independent weights and confidence thresholds.

SoccerMaster is a unified architecture that handles detection and field-line regression in a single forward pass. When selected, the separate detector-weights and keypoint-model controls are hidden because the unified model replaces both.

title: Detector weights
summary: The detector decides which players, referees, and ball candidates exist at all. Every downstream stage depends on it.
body: In this repo, `soccana` is the football-specific detector path. It is preferred because the app is tuned around football broadcast footage rather than generic COCO-style scenes.

If the detector misses small distant players, confuses referees, or hallucinates crowd and board boxes, the tracker, team clustering, and ball coverage all inherit that damage. Detector swaps are useful only when they improve the overlay, not because they sound more advanced.

title: Player tracker
summary: The tracker turns frame-local detections into identities that survive motion, occlusion, and re-entry.
body: `bytetrack` is the fast baseline. It follows detections frame to frame, but it does not try to reconnect long identity gaps once a player disappears or the scene changes too aggressively.

`hybrid_reid` adds sparse appearance embeddings and a stitch pass. That gives you two identity layers: raw online IDs from the live tracker and canonical stitched IDs after the merge pass. Raw-vs-stitched counts are useful because they tell you whether the post-pass is cleaning up tracker churn or inventing over-confident merges.
Use the overlay and the raw-vs-stitched metrics together. A lower stitched ID count is only good if the video still looks coherent when players cross, crowd, or leave and re-enter the frame.

links.label: Long-term player tracking with graph hierarchies
Team-Aware Football Player Tracking with SAM

title: Include ball tracking
summary: Ball tracking is a supporting signal for match context, not the primary success condition for the pipeline.
body: The ball is small, fast, heavily occluded, and often ambiguous against socks, feet, or ad boards. Good player coverage can coexist with weak ball continuity.
Use ball output to judge whether a clip reads like real play and to sanity-check events, but do not let a weak ball trace distract you from fixing detection, tracking, or calibration first.

title: Confidence and IOU thresholds
summary: These values control how aggressively the runtime accepts detections and how permissive box matching is.
body: Higher confidence usually removes more false positives but can also erase small distant players and the ball. Lower confidence recovers recall, but it can flood the tracker with garbage detections that create churn.

IOU settings affect how readily overlapping boxes are treated as the same object. Threshold tuning is meaningful only after you inspect the overlay; the right values depend on the clip scale, crowding, and camera motion.

title: Automatic field registration
summary: Field calibration refreshes from pitch keypoints and quality gates. It can fail even when player boxes look healthy.
body: This pipeline refreshes pitch geometry from field keypoints, then accepts or rejects each candidate homography based on visibility and geometric quality. A run can have good detections and still lose minimap quality if calibration refreshes keep failing.

The review metrics around calibration success, stale recovery, gate rejects, and projection stages are there to show whether the minimap is using fresh geometry, coasting on stale state, or being blocked entirely.

Treat field registration as a live runtime component, not a cosmetic post-process. If calibration is weak, projected player positions and ball locations should be considered suspect even when image-space boxes look stable.

title: SoccerNet browsing and download
summary: SoccerNet is the benchmark and dataset surface behind much of the football CV ecosystem this app borrows from.
body: This section is not just a file picker. It is the bridge between the local app and current football-specific benchmark tasks such as action spotting, calibration, tracking, and game-state reconstruction.

Use it when you want current benchmark footage and labels without manually copying paths every session. `Labels-v2.json` remains important because several higher-level experiments and event-aligned analyses expect that structure.

title: Scan a local dataset folder
summary: Folder scan is a convenience layer for turning a local dataset tree into clickable clip and label choices.
body: Use this when your footage already lives on disk and you want the app to surface candidate videos and annotation files without manually retyping full paths.
This is operational help, not a model or benchmark concept, so there are no external references here.

title: Input Clip
summary: This panel is the raw source video. It is where you verify what the model is seeing before trusting any overlay.
body: Use the raw clip to compare against live preview and saved overlay outputs. If the source itself is confusing or cropped badly, every downstream artifact will be harder to interpret.
When something looks wrong in review, come back here first and confirm that the failure is not caused by the clip segment, framing, or source mismatch.

title: Live Inference Preview
summary: Live preview is the fast visual debugging surface for runtime behavior before you commit to a full saved run.
body: Use this mode to catch detector misses, ID churn, and calibration dropouts quickly. It is deliberately lighter than a full analysis run so you can iterate before waiting for saved outputs.
If the preview already looks weak, do not expect the saved review to rescue it. Fix the earliest broken stage first, then rerun.

title: Saved Run Review
summary: Review mode is where you judge the saved overlay, diagnostics, and exported metrics for one completed run.
body: The review surface should answer two questions quickly: did this run actually work, and which pipeline stage needs attention first if it did not.
Use the overlay as the primary artifact. Metrics and diagnostics are there to explain what the video is already showing, not to replace it with abstract counters.

title: Training Studio
summary: Training Studio scans a local detector dataset, runs a fine-tune, then lets you review and activate the resulting checkpoint for analysis.
body: This surface is detector-only on purpose. It keeps dataset validation, local fine-tuning, job review, and registry activation in one loop.
Use it when the stock `soccana` checkpoint does not fit your camera angle or footage domain. A successful run should improve the downstream analysis overlay, not just create a new file.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage

title: Active detector
summary: This is the checkpoint the analysis workspace will use whenever the detector selector stays on `soccana`.
body: A finished job is not live until its registry entry is activated. A checkpoint can exist on disk and still have no effect on analysis.
If analysis behavior changes unexpectedly, check this first. The visible detector selector can still say `soccana` while a custom checkpoint is active underneath it.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage

title: Dataset intake
summary: Dataset intake validates a local YOLO detection dataset before the studio enables fine-tuning.
body: The scan checks split structure, label files, class names, and the football class contract the analysis pipeline expects.
If intake is wrong, fix the dataset first. Hyperparameter tuning will not rescue missing splits or bad player, ball, or referee IDs.

title: Dataset path
summary: Point this at the detector dataset root, not a single image folder or an output directory.
body: The studio expects a YOLO-style dataset layout it can scan and turn into a run-local manifest. If the root is wrong, the scan fails before training starts.
The source dataset stays in place. The worker writes its own generated runtime YAML inside the training run folder.

title: Scan result
summary: The scan result is a readiness report for the actual training contract, not just a file-system summary.
body: This panel explains whether the dataset is coherent enough to start detector fine-tuning safely. It should make clear what the studio found, what it had to infer, and what would make the resulting metrics less trustworthy.

A green result means the studio believes the split structure, label files, and class mapping are coherent enough to proceed. A warning means training may still work, but the operator should understand the compromise. A blocking result means the worker should not start.
A blocking result can also mean the path is a different dataset format entirely, such as a FiftyOne export with JSON annotations instead of a YOLO detector dataset with per-image label text files.
Use this panel to decide whether to fix data quality first. If the scan result looks weak, the right answer is usually in dataset structure or labels, not in hyperparameter tuning.

title: Validation strategy
summary: This tells you exactly where validation images come from for the run, which determines how honest the reported metrics can be.
body: `existing_split` means the dataset already had validation images and the studio keeps them. `generate_from_train` means the backend deterministically peels a validation list out of the discovered train images. `reuse_train_single_image` means the same image is used for both train and val because there is only one sample.

Those strategies are not equivalent. Compare metrics across runs only after checking this field, because a good score on a recycled or improvised validation slice should carry less weight than the same score on a real split.

title: Class mapping
summary: This is the semantic bridge from dataset class IDs to the football roles the workbench actually uses downstream.
body: The scanner looks for label hints that mean player or goalkeeper, ball, and referee. Goalkeeper is intentionally folded into player-side identity handling because the analysis pipeline needs a usable football person class more than it needs a separate keeper workflow here.
If these IDs are wrong, the checkpoint can train successfully and still be operationally broken. A ball class mapped to the wrong ID or a missing player mapping poisons analysis even if mAP goes up.

title: Training family
summary: Training family keeps the studio scoped to the detector workflow that the app supports today.
body: Right now the active family is detector fine-tuning only. That keeps the form, jobs view, and registry aligned with the current backend.
If more model families arrive later, they should be added as distinct workflows instead of overloading this one.

title: Detector fine-tuning
summary: Detector fine-tuning is the controlled workflow that turns a validated football detector dataset into a candidate checkpoint plus enough evidence to decide whether it belongs in analysis.
body: This form is where dataset validation, runtime defaults, and promotion rules become a real training run. It should feel like the operator-facing contract for how this repo adapts detectors, not like a thin shell-command wrapper.

A good fine-tune run should leave behind more than weights. It should leave a readable story: what dataset was used, what runtime trained it, what the metrics looked like, and why the resulting checkpoint should or should not become active.
That is the standard to hold this workflow to. If the run cannot teach the operator what happened, it is not yet a high-quality training surface.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage
Insights on Model Evaluation and Fine-Tuning

title: Base weights
summary: This chooses the checkpoint the fine-tune starts from. In this studio, that starting point is the football prior for players, ball, and referees.
body: `soccana` is not just a default label. It already knows football broadcast object scale and class structure, so the run starts from a detector that has seen the kinds of players, keepers, referees, and ball examples this workbench cares about.

Changing this field changes how much relearning the model must do before it becomes useful. A football-pretrained base usually recovers tiny-ball and distant-player behavior much faster than a generic checkpoint would.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage

title: Run name
summary: This is the human label that follows the run through Jobs and Registry. It is how you remember what changed without opening config files.
body: The backend still gives each run a timestamped `run_id`, but this field becomes the readable name shown on the job card and the registry entry. It is the operator-facing identity of the experiment.

Use it to encode the camera domain, dataset revision, or experiment intent, such as `wide-cam-soccernet-v2` or `broadcast-low-angle-ball-recall`. Good names make later promotion decisions much easier to audit.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage
Insights on Model Evaluation and Fine-Tuning

title: Training hyperparameters
summary: These controls shape how long the run trains, how much fits per step, and how aggressively the checkpoint adapts.
body: Epochs, image size, batch, workers, patience, and freeze trade runtime cost against adaptation strength and stability.
Start with a smaller smoke run when you are validating a new dataset. It is cheaper to catch bad labels or class mapping early.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage

title: Training device
summary: This is the requested hardware path for training. `auto` is only a request; the worker resolves the actual device when the run starts.
body: In the current backend, `auto` resolves in a simple order: MPS first if available, then CUDA, then CPU. That makes this field a practical runtime choice, not just a convenience toggle.

The device changes throughput, memory ceiling, and failure mode. CPU is slower but conservative. MPS and CUDA are faster when healthy, but they are also where memory and backend-specific instability show up first.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage

title: Runtime behavior
summary: This explains what the worker writes while the run is active and why those files matter after training ends.
body: Each run gets its own folder under `backend/training_runs/<run_id>/`. The worker writes a generated `dataset_runtime.yaml`, split text files, `progress.json`, `train.log`, Ultralytics output under `yolo_output/train/`, a copied `weights/best.pt`, a top-level `summary.json`, and a compact `training_provenance.json`.

When you activate a detector, the studio also copies the checkpoint into `backend/models/promoted/custom_<run_id>/` and writes matching provenance there. That gives Registry a stable promotion target instead of relying only on the transient run folder.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage
Insights on Model Evaluation and Fine-Tuning

title: Training jobs
summary: The Jobs tab is the run-by-run audit surface for detector training, and it should teach what happened, what it cost, and whether the checkpoint is activation-worthy.
body: Read a job card top to bottom. The metadata tells you what was trained and where. The progress and epoch state tell you how far the run got. The metrics tell you what the validation split thinks happened. The artifacts and logs tell you whether that story is trustworthy.

A finished job is not automatically a good job. If the dataset contract was weak, if the resolved device fell back in an unexpected way, or if the metrics look good but the overlay later looks worse, the right move is to reject or rerun the checkpoint instead of promoting it blindly.
Use the Jobs tab to compare evidence, not just completion state. The goal is to understand whether this run is a candidate detector for the product, not simply whether the worker exited successfully.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage
Insights on Model Evaluation and Fine-Tuning

title: AI training review
summary: This is the run-level narrative that turns config, logs, metrics, dataset contract, and artifacts into one activation-focused explanation.
body: The goal of this review is not to replace raw logs or metrics. It is to connect them into a clear answer to two questions: what actually happened in this run, and should this checkpoint move closer to activation for analysis.

A strong review should separate runtime failures from weak validation, weak validation from dataset-contract issues, and dataset-contract issues from artifact-readiness or promotion problems. Those are different problems and they need different fixes.
Read the summary line first, then the five collapsible sections. The sections are intentionally stable categories so you can compare runs without guessing which evidence bucket matters most.

links.label: Insights on Model Evaluation and Fine-Tuning

title: Run outcome
summary: This section says where the run actually ended and whether the result is a model-quality signal or a runtime failure signal.
body: A failed run at `0 / 50` epochs is not a weak detector result. It is usually a worker, device, callback, or training-loop execution problem. That difference matters because the right fix is in implementation or runtime configuration, not in more hyperparameter tweaking.
A completed run can still be a poor activation candidate, but it at least produced real optimization evidence. This section is there to stop those two situations from being blended together.

title: Dataset contract
summary: This section explains whether the run started from a trustworthy football detector dataset contract or from warning-heavy fallback logic.
body: Training Studio does more than count files. It checks class names, football role mapping, split structure, missing labels, and how validation images are being sourced.

A run can complete and still be operationally suspect if the dataset YAML names are weak, player or ball IDs are wrong, or the validation split had to be improvised. This section keeps that contract visible so you do not over-trust clean-looking metrics.

title: Training dynamics
summary: This section is about whether the loss and optimizer traces reflect real learning or only a partial worker startup before failure.
body: Sparse curve samples can mean very different things. On a healthy run, they are a coarse story of how the optimizer behaved. On a failed run, they may only prove that callback hooks fired before the loop stabilized.

Use this section to avoid reading too much into noisy or truncated curves. If epoch one never completed, the right interpretation is often `no valid learning signal yet`, not `interesting early overfit`.

links.label: Machine Learning Best Practices and Tips for Model Training
Insights on Model Evaluation and Fine-Tuning

title: Validation signal
summary: This section explains how believable the reported detector metrics are for this specific run.
body: Detector metrics only mean what the validation contract allows them to mean. A real validation split carries more weight than a generated fallback, and missing `results.csv` metrics after a crash do not tell you anything about detector quality at all.
Use this section to decide whether the numbers are evidence for promotion, evidence for caution, or simply unavailable because the worker never produced them.

links.label: Insights on Model Evaluation and Fine-Tuning

title: Artifact readiness
summary: This section is the bridge from a finished run folder to a checkpoint that the product can safely consider for activation.
body: A detector is not activation-ready just because training wrote a file. This section ties together checkpoint existence, summary state, provenance, and whether the run should move forward or stay blocked.

It matters because Training Studio is a product surface, not just a folder browser. The operator needs to know whether the run produced durable evidence, a promotable checkpoint, or only a useful failure record.

title: Training analysis prompt context
summary: This is the exact evidence bundle that was sent to the model when the AI training review was generated.
body: The prompt context is there for auditability. It shows the important log chunks, recent log tail, code excerpts, and budget metadata used to build the AI review.

Use it when the review seems too vague or surprisingly specific. It tells you whether the model had enough evidence, whether the right log block made it into context, and which implementation files were quoted for the diagnosis.

title: Run artifacts
summary: Run artifacts are the files that let you audit or reuse a training job after the process exits.
body: Different artifact rows answer different questions. A generated dataset manifest tells you what `data=` contract the worker really trained on. Curves, CSVs, and plots show how optimization moved. Logs explain runtime decisions and failures. Checkpoints are the files you can actually promote.

The provenance artifact matters because it ties the dataset path, run-local manifest, promoted checkpoint, and DVC-tracked or non-tracked state into one compact record. If a run is worth keeping, the artifact set should be enough to reconstruct what happened without guessing from folder names alone.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage

title: Detector registry
summary: The registry is the promotion layer that decides which detector the analysis workspace actually trusts.
body: A completed run becomes a registry candidate, not an automatic default. The registry is where the operator compares lineage, runtime, metrics, class compatibility, promotion path, and artifact trail before changing the detector that analysis will use.

This matters because activation is a product decision, not just a training outcome. A detector can finish training cleanly and still be the wrong default if its metrics are misleading or its football behavior regresses in the overlay.

Read the registry as a shortlist of candidates plus one active choice. The quality bar is not just whether the checkpoint exists, but whether it is the right checkpoint to push into day-to-day analysis and whether its provenance is durable enough to trust later.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage

title: Runtime profile
summary: This is the host-level context behind the training recommendations the studio is making on this machine.
body: The ribbon is describing real runtime facts: backend stack, version, preferred device, and which acceleration paths are actually available right now. On one machine that may mean a Mac-first MPS flow; on another it may mean CUDA or CPU only.
Use it to calibrate expectations before you start a run. The same form behaves differently on an MPS laptop, a CUDA box, and a CPU-only host.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage

title: Detector training metrics
summary: These are the validation-split detector metrics surfaced from the training backend. They matter only if you read them in football workflow terms.
body: Precision tells you how often predicted football objects are actually right. Recall tells you how many real players, referees, and ball instances the model is catching. `mAP50` is the easier overlap score, while `mAP50-95` is stricter about box quality.

In this product, a higher number is not automatically a better detector. Extra recall is bad if it floods tracking with junk boxes, and a good `mAP50` can still hide sloppy boxes that hurt player association and ball stability.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage
Insights on Model Evaluation and Fine-Tuning

title: Epochs
summary: This sets the maximum number of full passes over the generated training split before the run finishes or early stopping cuts it short.
body: More epochs give the checkpoint more time to adapt, but they also give a weak dataset more time to overfit. In this workflow, a short smoke run is often the fastest way to prove that labels, class mapping, and runtime are sane before spending a longer budget.
Read epochs as a cap on training effort, not as a quality guarantee. If the scan contract or validation strategy is weak, raising epochs usually magnifies a bad setup instead of rescuing it.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage
Machine Learning Best Practices and Tips for Model Training

title: Image size
summary: This is the training resolution. It directly affects how much detail the detector keeps for far-side players and the tiny ball.
body: Football datasets punish low resolution because many important objects are small: wide-angle players near the touchline, the ball in flight, and crowded box play. A larger `imgsz` preserves more of that structure.
The cost is immediate: higher memory use, slower steps, and usually a smaller sustainable batch size. In this studio, `imgsz` and `batch` should be tuned together, not independently.

title: Batch
summary: This controls how many images the trainer processes per optimization step after the runtime dataset manifest has been built.
body: Batch size is usually the first setting that collides with local hardware limits. Raising it can improve throughput and smooth out updates, but only if the chosen device can actually hold that many images at the selected `imgsz`.
If runs start failing on MPS or CUDA, batch is one of the first places to look. If `imgsz` goes up, the workable batch size usually comes down.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage
Machine Learning Best Practices and Tips for Model Training

title: Workers
summary: This sets how many host-side data loader workers feed images and labels into the trainer.
body: Workers do not change detector semantics. They change how hard the CPU and storage subsystem work to keep the training device busy.

On a fast local SSD, a few workers can keep MPS or CUDA fed smoothly. On a constrained laptop or slow disk, too many workers can create extra contention and make the whole machine feel worse without speeding training up.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage
Machine Learning Best Practices and Tips for Model Training

title: Patience
summary: This is the early-stopping tolerance: how many non-improving validation epochs the run will accept before stopping.
body: Patience only means something relative to the validation strategy. With a real validation split, it helps stop flat or regressing runs before they waste more local compute.

With fallback validation, especially `generate_from_train` or `reuse_train_single_image`, the signal is weaker. In that case, patience can still stop the run, but the decision is based on less trustworthy evidence.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage
Insights on Model Evaluation and Fine-Tuning

title: Freeze
summary: This decides how much of the pretrained detector stays locked instead of adapting to your dataset.
body: Higher freeze values preserve more of `soccana`'s football behavior and reduce drift. Lower values let the run rewrite more of the model so it can adapt harder to a new camera angle or annotation style.

That trade-off matters in football. Unfreezing too aggressively on a small or noisy dataset can damage useful pretrained behavior such as ball sensitivity or class separation, even if training appears to proceed normally.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage
Machine Learning Best Practices and Tips for Model Training

title: Cache images
summary: This changes how the worker reads data, not what the model learns. It trades extra local memory or disk pressure for less repeated file I/O.
body: When caching helps, the trainer spends less time waiting on image reads and more time actually stepping the model. That can make local iteration smoother, especially on slower storage.
When caching hurts, it simply moves the bottleneck into memory pressure or disk churn. It does not fix bad labels, weak class mapping, or a poor validation split.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage
Machine Learning Best Practices and Tips for Model Training

title: Dataset YAML
summary: This is the source dataset contract the scanner found. The worker later turns it into a run-local `dataset_runtime.yaml` that training actually consumes.
body: The scan uses the source YAML to discover split references and class names. If that file is missing, unparseable, or semantically wrong, the studio cannot safely map the trained detector back into analysis.

If the path points at a different export format, such as a FiftyOne dataset dump with JSON annotations, this field stays missing because there is no compatible detector-training YAML contract to trust.

The generated runtime manifest is only as good as the contract it starts from. If the source YAML points at the wrong splits or names classes badly, the run can still produce artifacts while learning the wrong thing.

title: Class source
summary: This is the provenance of the class names the studio is using to interpret the dataset.
body: The value is not decorative metadata. It tells you whether class names came from a real YAML path, whether that YAML existed but could not be parsed, or whether the source is missing entirely.

That matters because this product does not just need any class list. It needs names that can be mapped back into football roles the runtime understands, especially player or goalkeeper, ball, and referee.

title: Backend runtime
summary: This identifies the training implementation and version that actually produced the checkpoint you are looking at.
body: Today that means the local Ultralytics YOLO stack and its installed version, not an abstract idea of 'YOLO training'. The backend determines callback behavior, metric extraction, artifact layout, and what the logs mean.
If two runs come from materially different backend versions, compare them more cautiously. Even when the metric names look the same, the runtime that produced them is part of the evidence.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage

title: Resolved device
summary: This is the hardware path the worker actually used after resolving the requested device value.
body: For runs started with `auto`, this is the authoritative answer. In the current worker, the resolution order is MPS, then CUDA, then CPU.
Trust this field over the form input when you explain speed, memory behavior, or instability. It is execution provenance, not operator intent.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage

title: Epoch progress
summary: This shows how far the run got through its planned schedule as reported by `progress.json`.
body: It is more than a progress bar. It tells you whether the checkpoint reached enough training maturity to judge, or whether the run ended too early to mean much.
Use it with status and logs. `7 / 50` can mean a healthy early-stop decision, a manual stop, or a failure that happened before the run was ready for promotion.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage
Insights on Model Evaluation and Fine-Tuning

title: mAP50 and related detector metrics
summary: This is the forgiving detector quality score in the studio: predictions count as correct at a 0.50 IoU threshold on the validation split.
body: `mAP50` is useful because it rises early when the model starts finding the right football objects at all. It is often the quickest sanity signal that the run is not completely lost.

It is also easy to over-trust. A detector can score better at `mAP50` while still drawing loose boxes that are bad for tracking and ball localization, so always read it beside `mAP50-95`, precision, recall, and an actual overlay check.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage
Insights on Model Evaluation and Fine-Tuning

title: Checkpoint
summary: This is the concrete model file the product can activate, usually the copied `weights/best.pt` for the run.
body: Once a run is promoted, this exact path is what analysis and live preview will load when the active detector points at the custom checkpoint. It is the operational artifact, not just a breadcrumb to the training folder.
A checkpoint path by itself does not prove the run was good. Its value comes from the summary, class mapping, device provenance, and overlay behavior that justify trusting that file.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage

title: Run summary artifact
summary: This is the machine-readable record of the training run that the studio reopens later instead of scraping raw logs.
body: `summary.json` ties together config, dataset scan snapshot, generated dataset manifest, validation strategy, resolved device, metrics, curves, artifact paths, and checkpoint outcome.

The summary is the broad run contract. `training_provenance.json` is the narrower lineage contract for durable artifacts. Together they let Jobs and Registry stay product-like without scraping logs or inferring promotion history from paths.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage

title: Durable training artifacts
summary: Durable artifacts are the files that should still make sense after the original training process and local shell session are long gone.
body: A local run folder is useful, but activation is where the artifact needs to become trustworthy for the product. In this studio, that means copying the active checkpoint into `backend/models/promoted/custom_<run_id>/` and writing a compact provenance file beside it.

DVC is the optional durability layer around that. When a dataset folder or promoted checkpoint has a `.dvc` pointer, the studio can show whether the artifact is just a local path or a versioned asset you can restore and compare later.

title: DVC runtime status
summary: This tells you whether the repository is configured for DVC and whether the local CLI is available to restore or inspect tracked artifacts.
body: A `ready` state means both sides exist: the repo has `.dvc/` metadata and the local machine has a usable DVC binary. `repo configured` means the repo is prepared, but the local CLI is missing. `CLI only` means DVC exists on the machine, but this repo has not been initialized for it.

This matters because a missing CLI changes what the studio can guarantee. The run can still train, but the post-checkout restore hook and any DVC-backed artifact recovery will not actually execute on that machine.

title: Training provenance
summary: Training provenance is the compact lineage record that says which dataset, manifest, checkpoint, and promotion action produced the detector you are looking at.
body: The provenance file is intentionally narrower than the full summary. It keeps the exact dataset path, generated runtime manifest, checkpoint path, activation metadata, and DVC-tracked or non-tracked state together so the operator can audit lineage quickly.

Use it to answer the uncomfortable but important questions: was this checkpoint promoted from the run I think it was, is the dataset path the expected one, and are these assets durable or just local leftovers on one machine.

title: Canonical benchmark clip
summary: The canonical benchmark clip is the fixed video used only by the operational review lane, not by every labeled suite.
body: The operational lane still needs one fixed clip so overlay review remains apples-to-apples across recipes.

Labeled suites such as detection, tracking, calibration, or game-state use their own datasets and manifests instead of this clip cache. That is why the workbench now separates operational review from benchmark truth.

Pick a clip that exercises the failure modes you actually care about: far-side players, tight crowds, fast ball transitions, or wide-angle distortion. Treat it as supporting evidence, not as the paper-style benchmark authority.

title: Locked runtime profile
summary: Benchmark runs still record runtime context, but v2 compares suite-and-recipe pairs instead of only swapping one detector under one frozen clip setup.
body: The benchmark still captures runtime context such as device choice, evaluator versions, and the operational clip state when that lane is active.

What changed in v2 is that the benchmark surface is no longer pretending the only variable is a detector checkpoint. Recipes can encode detector, tracker, keypoint, and bundled-pipeline composition, while each suite defines its own metric contract.
The point is still to avoid ambiguous comparisons. Runtime context should explain the run, not collapse unlike task families into one scoreboard.

title: Benchmark candidate
summary: Legacy benchmark candidate language now maps to assets and recipes in Benchmark Lab v2.
body: An asset is a concrete model or pipeline artifact. A recipe is the runnable benchmark row that binds assets together into one executable comparison unit.
Local and Hugging Face imports still matter, but they now enter the system as assets first so compatibility and recipe generation stay honest.
If you still see the word candidate in an older artifact or legacy record, read it as pre-v2 language rather than the current product model.

links.label: Multi-class detection and tracking in soccer broadcast footage

title: Composite benchmark score
summary: Composite benchmark score is legacy operational language and is no longer the truth for Benchmark Lab v2.
body: V2 does not use one house score across detection, tracking, calibration, spotting, and game-state tasks.
If you are reading a legacy operational record, the old heuristic sub-metrics may still appear as supporting evidence for clip review.
For current benchmark truth, read the suite-specific metrics directly and keep each metric family in its own task lane.

title: Benchmark leaderboard
summary: Benchmark Lab v2 replaces the old leaderboard with a sortable, filterable results matrix focused on one suite at a time.
body: Use the active suite, presets, and filters to keep one honest task family in view. Rows are recipes and unsupported combinations stay visible as N/A.
This is intentionally not a global cross-task leaderboard. Detection AP, tracking HOTA, calibration JaC@5, spotting F1, and game-state GS-HOTA should not compete inside one synthetic super-score.

If you are reopening a legacy operational record, the old leaderboard language may appear only as historical context. The current product surface should be read as a matrix, not a detector-only ranking board.

title: Import a local checkpoint
summary: Use a `.pt` file from anywhere on disk as a benchmark candidate without promoting or registering it first.
body: This is the fastest way to benchmark a checkpoint that exists on your machine but has not been through the Training Studio promotion flow. Point at the file, give it a label, and the benchmark runner handles the rest.

The benchmark does not copy or move the file. It reads the checkpoint in place. If the file disappears or changes between candidate creation and benchmark execution, the run will fail for that candidate.
Use this when you have a checkpoint from an external training pipeline, a colleague, or an earlier experiment and want to see how it compares against the current active detector on the same clip.

title: Import from Hugging Face
summary: Pull a detector checkpoint from a Hugging Face model repository and add it as a benchmark candidate.
body: This source downloads the model weights by Hugging Face repo ID and caches them locally before running the benchmark. It is useful for comparing community-published football detectors, team-shared checkpoints, or upstream model revisions without manual file management.
The download happens once per unique model ID. Subsequent benchmark runs that reference the same ID reuse the cached weights.

Not every Hugging Face model will work. The checkpoint must be compatible with the Ultralytics YOLO loader that this workbench uses for inference. If the format is wrong, the candidate run will fail with a loader error rather than producing misleading scores.

title: Track stability
summary: Track stability measures how consistently the detector feeds the tracker with usable identities across the benchmark clip.
body: A detector that produces many detections per frame but causes frequent ID switches, short-lived tracks, or excessive tracklet merges will score low here. Stability reflects the downstream tracker experience, not just raw box count.
The metric combines average track length, stitched-to-raw ID reduction ratio, and tracklet merge density into a single normalized score. Longer tracks with fewer forced merges score higher.

This is the metric that most directly predicts whether the overlay will look coherent. A detector with high mAP but low track stability is producing boxes that the tracker cannot maintain as consistent identities.

links.label: Long-term player tracking with graph hierarchies
Team-Aware Football Player Tracking with SAM

title: Calibration health
summary: Calibration health measures how reliably the field registration pipeline produces fresh, valid homographies during the benchmark clip.
body: Field calibration depends on visible pitch keypoints, which in turn depend on the detector not flooding the frame with noise that confuses the keypoint model. A detector that hallucinates crowd boxes or misses the pitch area can indirectly degrade calibration even though the field model itself is unchanged.

The metric uses the ratio of successful calibration refreshes to total refresh attempts, combined with the average visible pitch keypoints per frame. Higher success rates and more keypoints mean healthier calibration.

If a candidate scores well on coverage but poorly on calibration health, the detector may be finding players but degrading the minimap and projection layers. That trade-off matters for any downstream analysis that depends on field coordinates.

title: Detection coverage
summary: Coverage measures how many players and ball instances the detector finds per frame on average across the benchmark clip.
body: In football broadcast footage, a good detector should find most visible players on both teams, the referee crew, and the ball when it is in frame. Coverage counts how many of those the detector actually produces.

The metric normalizes average player detections per frame and average ball detections per frame against expected ranges for broadcast football. A detector that consistently finds 18-22 players per frame in a wide shot scores higher than one that finds 8.

Coverage alone does not prove quality. A detector that hallucinates boxes also produces high raw counts. Use coverage together with track stability to distinguish real recall from noisy over-detection.

title: Benchmark suites
summary: Suites define the task, dataset, metrics, and capability contract for a benchmark comparison.
body: A suite is the benchmark protocol itself, not just a dataset name. It declares which metrics matter, which capabilities are required, and what evidence counts as a valid result.
Selecting multiple suites means the same recipe set is judged across more than one benchmark family. Unsupported combinations stay visible instead of being silently dropped.
Use the suite selector to choose what evidence you actually want, then use the matrix to inspect one suite family at a time.

title: Benchmark run controls
summary: Run controls describe the selected matrix, start benchmark jobs, and let you reopen prior benchmark records.
body: The run controls area tells you how many selected suite-by-recipe cells are runnable now and how many are blocked by compatibility, missing datasets, or unavailable assets.
Starting a benchmark records the selected suites and recipes as a persisted run. Reopening an earlier run lets you inspect historical results through the same matrix and detail surfaces.
Treat this area as the execution desk for the matrix, not as the explanation of the metrics themselves.

title: Recipe browser
summary: The recipe browser is where you choose runnable benchmark recipes rather than loose model files.
body: A recipe already encodes how detector, tracker, keypoint, and bundled pipeline pieces fit together for benchmarking. That is why the matrix compares recipes, not arbitrary asset fragments.
Imported checkpoints become assets first, then recipes. This keeps the benchmark contract consistent even when models come from different sources.
Recipe compatibility is honest: if the active suite requires capabilities a recipe does not expose, the matrix will show N/A instead of inventing a fallback.

title: Results matrix
summary: The results matrix focuses one suite at a time so recipes can be sorted and filtered on that suite's own metric family.
body: Pick an active suite, then compare recipes by the metrics that suite actually declares. This keeps tracking, calibration, detection, and spotting evidence from collapsing into one fake ranking.
Use presets and filters to slice the recipe set honestly. Unsupported rows remain visible as N/A so capability gaps are obvious.
The matrix is the main evidence surface for paper-style benchmark numbers, while operational overlay review stays available as a separate supporting lane.

title: Matrix filters and presets
summary: Filters and presets narrow the matrix to comparable rows without deleting incompatible recipes from the benchmark record.
body: Presets shift the view toward one suite family such as Detection, Tracking, or Game State. Filters narrow the active recipe set by provider, architecture, bundle mode, status, capability, or N/A behavior.
This is better than hiding incompatible recipes entirely because the operator can still see why a row does not belong in the current comparison slice.
Use these controls to compare like with like, not to manufacture a prettier table.

title: Benchmark detail panel
summary: The detail panel expands one suite-and-recipe selection into metrics, artifacts, composition, and compatibility evidence.
body: Use the detail panel when a matrix row looks interesting and you need to see the full metric breakdown, artifact paths, or which assets make up the recipe.
The panel is intentionally compatible with the matrix rather than replacing it. The matrix helps you compare; the detail panel helps you inspect one row honestly.
Operational overlay review remains complementary and can still be inspected beside the detail panel when the active suite is the operational lane.

title: Operational review lane
summary: Operational review keeps the overlay-first clip lane visible without pretending it is a labeled benchmark.
body: This lane is still useful because it shows how a recipe behaves inside the real workbench pipeline on a fixed clip: overlay quality, tracker stability, calibration health, and throughput.
It is supporting evidence, not the benchmark authority. The official suite metrics should decide the paper-style comparisons.
Keeping the operational lane separate prevents the workbench from mixing heuristic pipeline health with labeled benchmark truth.

title: Processing throughput
summary: Throughput measures how fast the detector drives the full pipeline in frames per second on the benchmark clip.
body: Speed matters because this workbench processes real video. A detector that is accurate but processes 2 FPS on your machine may be impractical for iterative work or longer clips.

The metric is the raw FPS reported by the analysis pipeline on this host with the locked runtime profile. It is hardware-dependent, so throughput comparisons are valid only between candidates run on the same machine.
Treat throughput as host-specific context and operational evidence, not as a replacement for the suite's declared primary metric. A fast bad detector is still bad.

title: Benchmark matrix view
summary: The Benchmark Lab matrix is a suite-focused comparison table that lets you sort and filter recipes within one honest task family at a time.
body: The matrix is intentionally suite-first. Pick an active suite, then compare recipes using that suite's own metric columns instead of inventing a cross-task score.
Use view presets and filters to keep unlike things apart. Detection AP, tracking HOTA, calibration JaC@5, and game-state GS-HOTA should not compete inside one synthetic super-score.
Unsupported combinations stay visible as N/A so the operator can see capability gaps without pretending those rows are directly comparable.

title: Detection Roles Quick
summary: This quick suite measures four-class football detection with COCO-style AP metrics on a small real-image dataset.
body: Use this as the fast sanity check for detector regressions on player, goalkeeper, ball, and referee boxes. It is intentionally small and cheap, so it is useful for iteration, not for declaring a final winner.
The scores are standard COCO-style detection metrics, with AP@[.50:.95] as the primary sort metric. Runtime columns are secondary host-specific context, not the main benchmark truth.
Because this dataset is small, treat it as the first gate in the ladder. Strong performance here should send a recipe to the larger SoccerNet suites, not end the comparison.

title: Detection Ball Quick
summary: This quick suite isolates football ball detection with COCO-style AP metrics so small-object regression is visible on its own.
body: Use this when a detector claims better ball behavior and you need to separate that from player or referee box quality.
Ball AP is harder and more volatile than player AP because the object is tiny, fast, and often motion-blurred or partially occluded.
This suite belongs in the quick gate. A promising result here should send the recipe to the heavier ladders, not finish the evaluation story.

title: SynLoc Quick
summary: This quick suite scores world-coordinate localization quality with mAP-LocSim rather than image-space box overlap.
body: Use this when you care about whether the recipe preserves useful pitch-space location after projection, not only whether it draws plausible boxes.
mAP-LocSim belongs to a localization task family. Keep it separate from AP, HOTA, and spotting metrics.
A strong result here is evidence that a recipe can support downstream tactical location work rather than just visual overlays.

title: Team BAS Quick
summary: This quick suite measures team-aware ball action spotting with Team-mAP@1.
body: Use this suite only for recipes that genuinely expose spotting outputs. If a recipe is detector-only or tracking-only, it should stay N/A here.
Team-mAP@1 answers a semantic timing-and-team assignment question, not a detection or tracking question.
This is a useful readiness gate for future event-capable recipes, even if today's catalog has few or no compatible rows.

title: Calibration Medium
summary: This medium suite measures camera calibration quality with the official Completeness x JaC@5 score.
body: Use this suite when geometric correctness matters for the downstream tactical output. It is the right benchmark for line/field registration quality, not detector AP.
A calibration metric should stay in its own family. Do not average it into tracking or detection leaderboards.
This is a medium-cost gate because calibration evaluation usually matters after the recipe already looks plausible on quick detection checks.

title: Tracking Medium
summary: This medium suite measures temporal tracking quality with HOTA-family metrics.
body: Use this suite to compare trackers and tracking-capable pipelines on identity continuity over time, not just per-frame detection quality.
HOTA, DetA, and AssA stay visible together because they explain whether a change helped detection coverage, association quality, or both.
This suite becomes meaningful only when the recipe really outputs tracking-capable results.

In the current repo state, this lane is still explicitly blocked until a real `SoccerNetMOT` tree is materialized and the workbench exports per-recipe prediction archives for the vendored `sn-tracking` evaluator instead of assuming a generic `evaluate.py` flow.

title: PCBAS Medium
summary: This medium suite measures person-centric ball action spotting with F1@15%.
body: Use this suite for person-centric spotting recipes, not for plain detectors or trackers that do not produce the required semantic outputs.
F1@15% belongs to the spotting family and should stay beside other spotting metrics instead of being blended into AP or HOTA.
This suite is part of the benchmark ladder even when today's catalog has honest N/A rows.

title: Game State Medium
summary: This medium suite is a fixed 12-clip SoccerNet GSR validation subset scored with GS-HOTA.
body: Use this suite when you want a serious benchmark that still finishes in a practical local time window. It is the main bridge between detector-centric experimentation and full tactical reconstruction.
GS-HOTA is the primary metric because it reflects end-to-end game-state quality over time rather than raw box overlap alone. HOTA, DetA, and AssA stay visible as supporting breakdowns.
This suite is DVC-managed through its manifest and dataset state so the medium benchmark stays reproducible instead of drifting with ad hoc clip choices.

In the current repo state, this lane is still explicitly blocked until the fixed 12-clip `SoccerNetGS` subset is materialized and the workbench runs the vendored TrackLab/sn-gamestate pipeline per recipe instead of assuming a generic `evaluate.py` entrypoint.

title: Game State Long
summary: This is the long full-validation SoccerNet GSR run. Use it for serious final comparisons, not for quick iteration.
body: The long suite exists so the workbench can produce a paper-style final comparison on a larger official benchmark instead of overfitting to a tiny smoke set or one favorite clip.
Because this run is much heavier, it should usually come after a recipe already looks promising on the quick and medium ladders.
The result still shows separate GS-HOTA, HOTA, DetA, and AssA columns. The long run is more evidence, not a license to collapse task boundaries into one house score.

title: Operational Review
summary: Operational Review keeps the old clip-based benchmark lane alive, but it is explicitly not a ground-truth benchmark.
body: This lane is still useful because it shows how a recipe behaves inside the actual workbench pipeline: tracker stability, calibration health, coverage, saved overlay output, and clip-speed behavior.
It is not a substitute for labeled evaluation. Use it to inspect overlays, failure modes, and operator-facing behavior after a recipe already looks credible on a real suite.
Keeping this lane separate prevents the product from mixing operational heuristics with official benchmark metrics.

title: AP@[.50:.95]
summary: AP@[.50:.95] is the standard COCO-style average precision across IoU thresholds from 0.50 to 0.95.
body: This is a stricter and more informative detector metric than AP50 alone because it rewards both finding objects and localizing them tightly.
In the Benchmark Lab v2 detection suites, this is the main benchmark sort key unless the active suite declares a different primary metric.
If AP50 looks great but AP@[.50:.95] drops sharply, the detector is probably finding objects but placing boxes too loosely for serious downstream use.

title: mAP-LocSim
summary: mAP-LocSim scores world-coordinate localization quality in the SynLoc task.
body: This is not an image-space detector metric. It measures how well a method localizes football actors on the pitch in world coordinates.
Use it to judge localization-ready recipes, not to compare directly against spotting or tracking metrics.
A strong mAP-LocSim score means the recipe is preserving useful spatial accuracy after projection, not just drawing believable boxes.

title: Team-mAP@1
summary: Team-mAP@1 is the main metric for team-aware ball action spotting.
body: This metric evaluates whether the action was spotted at the correct time and assigned to the correct team side. It belongs to spotting semantics, not tracking or calibration.
Do not compare Team-mAP@1 directly against AP or HOTA. They answer different benchmark questions.
If a recipe has no event-spotting capability, the matrix should show N/A rather than inventing a cross-task score.

title: Completeness x JaC@5
summary: Completeness x JaC@5 is the official calibration benchmark score for camera geometry quality.
body: This metric rewards calibration solutions that are both usable and geometrically accurate, rather than accepting a solution that only solves a few easy lines.
It is a calibration-specific metric and should stay in the calibration view or suite, not be averaged together with detection or spotting scores.
If calibration is important to the tactical workbench outcome, this is the benchmark signal to watch instead of image-space AP.

title: HOTA
summary: HOTA is a tracking metric that balances detection quality and association quality over time.
body: HOTA is useful because it does not let a tracker win purely by producing lots of detections or purely by associating a handful of obvious tracks well.
DetA and AssA remain visible beside it so you can see whether a change helped detection quality, association quality, or both.
Use HOTA-family metrics for temporal identity tasks. Do not treat them as substitutes for spotting or calibration metrics.

title: GS-HOTA
summary: GS-HOTA is the game-state reconstruction metric for SoccerNet GSR.
body: GS-HOTA is the most aligned official metric for this workbench’s tactical destination because it evaluates reconstruction quality over time rather than only image-space boxes.
Use it as the primary sort key for the GSR suites. Supporting columns such as HOTA, DetA, and AssA help explain why a recipe landed where it did.
This is the benchmark that should decide the serious long-form ranking once a recipe has already passed the quick and medium gates.

title: F1@15%
summary: F1@15% is the main FOOTPASS-style person-centric spotting metric.
body: This metric belongs to the person-centric spotting family. It checks whether the action was assigned correctly within the allowed temporal tolerance.
Because it answers a semantic action question, it should be read beside spotting-specific supporting metrics, not averaged with AP or HOTA.
If the active recipe does not produce person-centric spotting outputs, Benchmark Lab should display N/A here.

title: Not Applicable
summary: N/A means the selected recipe and suite do not form an honest benchmark pair.
body: The matrix intentionally uses N/A when a recipe lacks the required capability for a suite, or when the task boundary does not match the recipe’s outputs.
This is better than inventing fallback numbers or silently substituting another metric family.
Use filters and presets to compare like with like. The point of the matrix is to keep incompatible rows visible without pretending they are directly comparable.

title: Benchmark suites
summary: Suites define the task family, protocol, dataset expectations, and primary metric for a benchmark run.
body: A suite is not just a label. It fixes what is being measured, which metrics matter, and what capability contract a recipe must satisfy before the result is honest.
Selecting multiple suites means the same recipes can be run through multiple benchmark shapes, but the visible matrix still focuses on one suite at a time so the metric columns stay meaningful.
If a suite is missing its dataset or its operational clip, the workbench should say so explicitly instead of pretending the row is comparable.

title: Benchmark recipe browser
summary: Recipes are runnable benchmark bindings, not loose assets.
body: A recipe already encodes the detector, tracker, keypoint model, and pipeline binding that Benchmark Lab will execute for a suite.

This matters because the benchmark truth is about runnable systems, not a disconnected pile of checkpoints. Two recipes may share one detector but differ meaningfully in tracking or calibration behavior.
Importing a detector adds new assets and recipe rows, but a row only becomes benchmark-valid for a suite when its capability contract actually matches.

title: Benchmark run controls
summary: Run Controls launch the selected suite-by-recipe matrix and keep blocked cells visible before execution.
body: The counts here are the preflight truth: how many selected cells are runnable now, how many are blocked by missing datasets, and how many are incompatible by capability.
This prevents the operator from confusing 'not executed' with 'not supported'. Both states matter, but they mean different things for model comparison.
The selected benchmark history also rehydrates previous suite results so the matrix and detail panel can be reviewed without re-running everything.

title: Matrix filters and presets
summary: Presets narrow the task family, and filters narrow the recipe rows shown in the matrix.
body: Use presets to move between detection, spotting, localization, calibration, tracking, game state, and operational review contexts without pretending those tasks share one score.
Use filters to keep like with like: same provider, same architecture family, same bundle mode, same capability, or only rows that support the active suite.
The point of this toolbar is not cosmetic. It is how the product avoids turning a heterogeneous benchmark catalog into an unreadable table.

title: Results matrix
summary: The results matrix shows recipe rows against metric columns for the active suite.
body: This is intentionally not a global cross-task leaderboard. The columns change with the active suite so the visible numbers stay within one real evaluation protocol at a time.
Clicking a metric header changes the sort column. That makes it possible to compare the same recipe pool under different benchmark truths without inventing a house composite.
When a recipe does not support the active suite, the row should still remain visible if your filters allow it, but the metric cells should say N/A honestly.

title: Benchmark detail panel
summary: The detail panel is the drilldown for one recipe under one active suite.
body: Use this panel to inspect the selected row's metrics, artifact paths, composition, and benchmark logs without losing the matrix context.
The panel matters because a single scalar is never enough. A recipe can look good on the primary metric while still exposing weak artifacts, poor compatibility, or suspicious runtime evidence.
If a row is operational rather than ground-truth, the detail panel should make that distinction visible instead of letting the operator assume the evidence is label-backed.

title: Operational review
summary: Operational review is the overlay-first lane for comparing how recipes behave inside the real workbench pipeline.
body: This lane is deliberately preserved because overlays, calibration stability, and operator-visible behavior still matter even when they are not official benchmark numbers.
It must remain visually separate from label-backed suites so the operator does not confuse pipeline review with benchmark truth.
Use operational review after a recipe already looks credible on a real suite, or when you need to understand why a benchmarked recipe still feels bad in product use.

title: Detection Ball Quick
summary: This quick suite isolates football ball detection with COCO-style AP metrics.
body: Use this suite when ball visibility is the change you care about and player-role metrics would hide the regression.
The suite keeps ball detection inside a real AP family rather than collapsing it into one generic pipeline score.
Because the task is narrower than full player detection, treat it as a specialized quick gate, not the whole benchmark story.

title: Localization Quick
summary: This quick suite checks world-coordinate localization quality with mAP-LocSim.
body: Use it to judge whether a recipe preserves spatial accuracy after projection, not whether it merely draws plausible image-space boxes.
Localization metrics answer a different question from AP, HOTA, or spotting scores, so the matrix must keep this suite separate.
If a recipe cannot produce the required localization outputs, the row should stay visible as N/A rather than being forced into an image-space comparison.

title: Team Action Spotting Quick
summary: This quick suite measures team-aware ball action spotting with Team-mAP@1.
body: This is a spotting suite, not a detector suite. It belongs to event semantics and team attribution rather than box overlap alone.
Keep this lane visible even when no current recipe supports it, because the honest N/A is part of the capability story.
If a future recipe does support it, compare it inside this suite rather than pretending the score is interchangeable with AP or HOTA.

title: Calibration Medium
summary: This medium suite measures camera-geometry quality with Completeness x JaC@5.
body: Use this suite when projection and field registration quality are the real product bottlenecks, not raw detection.
A strong calibration score means the recipe is producing usable field geometry, not just surviving visually on one clip.
Because calibration answers a geometry question, do not blend it into detection or tracking scores.

title: Tracking Medium
summary: This medium suite measures temporal association quality with HOTA-family tracking metrics.
body: Use this suite when the difference between recipes is less about single-frame detection and more about identity continuity over time.
HOTA, DetA, and AssA stay together because they explain whether a change helped detection quality, association quality, or both.
Tracking suites are where tracker and ReID choices become benchmark-visible rather than only operationally visible.

title: Person-Centric Ball Action Spotting Medium
summary: This medium suite measures person-centric spotting quality with FOOTPASS-style F1@15%.
body: This suite is about semantic action timing and assignment, not image-space localization.
Keep it in the benchmark catalog even if current recipes cannot satisfy it yet. Honest N/A rows are better than silently erasing the task family.
When event-capable recipes arrive, this lane should remain its own spotting benchmark instead of being collapsed into a general detector score.

title: Benchmark Charts
summary: Benchmark Charts visualize the active suite with the same recipe pool, filters, and selection state as the matrix.
body: This section is where the matrix becomes interactive evidence instead of just a table. The charts stay locked to the active suite and visible recipe subset, so clicks and legend toggles never leave the benchmark context.

Use the line or bar mode to change presentation, not semantics. The underlying comparison still comes from the active suite's declared metric family and the same filtered recipe pool feeding the matrix.
The point of this block is to make differences legible: who leads on the current metric, who is expensive at runtime, and whether a recipe's shape stays strong across the suite's metric family.

title: Recipe Subset Spotlight
summary: The recipe subset controls hide or restore visible series without changing the persisted benchmark record.
body: The legend pills are a visual spotlight, not a data rewrite. They let the operator narrow the visible comparison pool so charts and the matrix can focus on a smaller set of recipes while leaving the underlying benchmark run intact.
Use this when the full recipe pool becomes noisy or when you want to compare a promoted custom detector against a few baselines without rerunning the benchmark.
Because the spotlight feeds both the charts and the matrix, it becomes a fast way to keep detail drilldowns synchronized while still preserving blocked or unsupported rows in the benchmark record.

title: Primary Metric Comparison
summary: This chart compares the active suite's declared primary metric across the visible recipe pool.
body: The active suite decides what the primary metric means. In detection that may be AP, in tracking it is HOTA, and in game state it is GS-HOTA. The chart deliberately does not invent a cross-task score.
Use this as the fastest visual ranking surface for the current suite, then confirm the same story in the matrix and detail panel before making a promotion decision.
Clicking a point or bar should move the detail panel to the same recipe so the chart remains part of the core benchmark workflow instead of becoming a detached report.

title: Runtime Burden
summary: Runtime burden shows how expensive the visible recipes are on the active suite's runtime-oriented metric.
body: Accuracy without runtime context can hide impractical wins. This chart keeps throughput or latency beside the primary metric so the operator can see whether a result is expensive, cheap, or surprisingly efficient.
Read runtime only within one machine context. It is valuable for local product decisions, but it is not a substitute for the suite's declared benchmark truth.
When a suite exposes no runtime metric, the chart should say so plainly instead of fabricating one from unrelated signals.

title: Metric Explorer
summary: The Metric Explorer changes which active-suite metric is spotlighted in the interactive comparison chart.
body: Use the dropdown when the primary metric alone is not enough and you want to inspect a supporting column such as latency, AssA, DetA, or another declared suite metric.
This keeps exploration honest because you are still choosing from the active suite's own metric family rather than switching to unrelated benchmark tasks.
Metric exploration is most useful after the suite and recipe subset are already narrowed, so the chart shows a meaningful comparison rather than a wall of barely related lines.

title: Metric Profile
summary: The metric profile normalizes each visible metric column so recipe shape is easy to compare across the active suite.
body: This chart is about relative shape, not raw magnitude. Each metric column is normalized within the current visible recipe pool so you can see whether one recipe is consistently strong or only winning on one metric.
It is useful when raw values live on very different scales, such as throughput versus HOTA breakdowns, and you still want a fast visual read on which recipe has the broadest strength.
Treat this as an explanatory companion to the raw table. Normalization improves comparability but should never replace the actual benchmark numbers in the matrix and detail panel.

title: Suite Evaluation Charts
summary: Suite Evaluation Charts compare the benchmark suites themselves across the current shared recipe pool.
body: This section asks which suites are informative, discriminatory, expensive, blocked, or only partially materialized. It is how Benchmark Lab V2 evaluates the benchmark datasets and protocols themselves, not only the models.
Use it to see whether a suite has enough comparable recipes, whether its primary metric meaningfully spreads the visible pool, and whether it agrees or disagrees with another suite's ranking.

A suite with high blocked rate or thin materialization coverage may still be important, but the product should surface that operational reality directly instead of pretending every suite is equally ready.

title: Discriminative Power
summary: Discriminative power summarizes how strongly a suite separates the visible recipe pool on its primary metric.
body: A suite with strong spread or dispersion is drawing clearer distinctions between recipes. That can make it a better benchmark for choosing among contenders, provided the comparable recipe pool is large enough to trust the signal.
This does not automatically mean the suite is better overall. A stable low-spread suite may still be valuable as a sanity check or contract gate.
Read discriminative power together with comparable recipe count and blocked rate so you do not mistake a dramatic result from a tiny or heavily blocked pool for a durable benchmark conclusion.

title: Rank Correlation
summary: Rank correlation measures how similarly two suites order the same shared recipe pool.
body: High rank correlation means two suites broadly agree on which visible recipes are stronger or weaker. Low correlation means the suites are rewarding different behaviour, which is often a useful finding rather than a defect.
This is one of the clearest ways to judge whether an operational lane and a label-backed suite are telling the same story or pulling in different directions.
Correlation is only meaningful when the suites share enough comparable recipes. If the shared pool is too small, the chart should surface N/A rather than pretending the agreement score is stable.

title: Suite Average
summary: Suite average is the mean primary-metric score across the visible comparable recipes for one suite.
body: This is a convenience summary of how the currently visible recipe pool performs on one suite's declared primary metric.

Use it with spread and dispersion. A strong average with no spread may mean the suite is easy for every visible recipe, while a lower average with strong spread may be more useful for separating contenders.
The average only includes comparable recipes with real metric values. Blocked or unavailable rows should stay visible elsewhere in the product instead of contaminating the mean.

title: Blocked Rate
summary: Blocked rate shows how much of the visible recipe pool cannot run honestly on a suite because of dataset, runtime, or evaluator blockers.
body: A high blocked rate does not make a suite worthless, but it does mean the operator should treat its current evidence as operationally thin.
This metric helps distinguish a genuinely ready benchmark from one that is still waiting on materialization, environment work, or a missing recipe-to-evaluator bridge.
Blocked rate belongs beside comparable recipe count and materialization coverage so readiness problems are visible before anyone overinterprets the remaining successful cells.

title: Unavailable Rate
summary: Unavailable rate shows how much of the visible recipe pool is missing assets or otherwise cannot even enter honest evaluation for a suite.
body: Use this to separate suite-level blockers from recipe-level availability problems. A recipe may be conceptually compatible with a suite but still unavailable because its assets are missing or broken.
This is especially helpful when comparing built-in assets, promoted checkpoints, and imported models in the same benchmark surface.
Unavailable rows should remain visible in the matrix and charts so the operator can see what is absent instead of assuming silence means support.

title: Failure Rate
summary: Failure rate captures cells that started evaluation but still ended in an execution failure rather than a clean blocker or completion.
body: A failed cell is different from a blocked or unavailable cell. Failure means the benchmark path tried to run and still broke, which is often the highest-priority operational problem in the surface.
Keep failure rate near blocked and unavailable rates so execution health is separated into honest categories instead of one vague red bucket.
When failure rate rises, inspect the detail panel and raw payload for the specific recipe and suite intersection before trusting any benchmark summary.

title: Materialization Coverage
summary: Materialization coverage is a readiness-oriented percentage that summarizes whether the suite's dataset and manifest are actually present for the current workspace.
body: This is not a benchmark accuracy score. It is a product-surface readiness signal that keeps data reality visible while the suite system spans quick, medium, and operational lanes.
A suite may be conceptually first-class while still having low materialization coverage on one machine. The product should show that directly rather than hiding the missing state behind empty charts.
Read this together with blocked rate and sample size so you can tell whether a suite is weak because the benchmark is uninformative or simply because the current workspace is not fully prepared.

title: Sample Size
summary: Sample size reports the manifest-sized item count currently declared for a suite when that information is available.
body: Sample size helps the operator judge whether a suite is a quick spot-check, a medium validation subset, or a deeper benchmark lane.
This should come from the suite's own manifest or materialization contract rather than an invented cross-task proxy.
When sample size is unavailable, the UI should say so plainly instead of fabricating a comparable number.

title: Comparable Recipes
summary: Comparable recipes counts how many visible recipes currently have a real primary-metric result for the suite.
body: This is one of the most important context metrics in suite evaluation. Many benchmark summaries become misleading when only one or two recipes are actually comparable.
Comparable count is the guardrail for reading spread, dispersion, and rank correlation honestly. Thin pools should be treated as provisional evidence.
Blocked, unavailable, or failed rows still matter operationally, but they should not be smuggled into the comparable pool.
